f: A young company trying to grow very fast, usually with technology.
c: Baby tribe with big dream. Small now, wants to be mammoth soon.

f: The most famous startup accelerator. Twice+ a year it funds a group of startups and coaches them for ~3 months.
c: Wise rich tribe. Gives baby tribes rocks + advice for small piece of tribe. Many famous tribes (Airbnb, Stripe) came from its cave.

f: One group of startups that goes through YC together, named by season and year — written as W26, X25, S25, F24.
c: One hunting season's group of baby tribes. W26 = the tribes that trained together in winter of 2026.

f: Shorthand for YC batches: W = Winter, X = Spring, S = Summer, F = Fall, plus the last two digits of the year. W26 means the Winter 2026 batch.
c: Little season marks. W = cold season, X = sprout season, S = hot season, F = leaf-fall season. W26 = cold season of 2026. Yes, X for spring is strange. YC picked it, not caveman.

f: The final day of a YC batch, when every startup pitches to a room full of investors.
c: Big show day. Every baby tribe stands on rock and yells 'give us rocks!' to crowd of rich humans.

f: A program that gives startups money, advice and connections for a few months in exchange for equity.
c: Training cave for baby tribes. Feed them, teach them, push them out stronger.

f: A person who starts a company.
c: Human who says 'me build new tribe' and actually does it.

f: A founder who starts the company together with others.
c: Hunt-buddy who starts tribe with you. Shares meat and pain.

f: Chief Executive Officer — the top decision-maker in a company.
c: Big chief. Decides where tribe hunts, answers when things go wrong.

f: Chief Technology Officer — leads the technical/engineering side.
c: Chief of tool-makers. Makes sure the magic actually works.

f: Business-to-business: a company that sells to other companies.
c: Tribe sells to other tribes, not to normal cave humans. Fewer buyers, bigger rocks each.

f: Business-to-consumer: a company that sells directly to regular people.
c: Tribe sells to normal cave humans. Many buyers, small rocks each.

f: Business-to-government: selling to government agencies.
c: Tribe sells to village chiefs. Slow to say yes, but chief rocks flow long time.

f: Software as a Service — software you rent monthly instead of buying once.
c: Rent-a-tool. You no buy hammer, you pay small rocks every moon to keep using hammer. Tribe loves this: rocks come forever.

f: A platform connecting buyers and sellers, taking a cut of each trade.
c: Trading ground tribe builds. Hunters meet buyers there; tribe takes small piece of every trade.

f: A base product that other people build things or businesses on top of.
c: Big flat rock others build huts on. Own the rock = very strong.

f: When a product gets more valuable as more people use it.
c: More humans at fire = warmer fire = even more humans come. Very strong magic, hard for enemy to copy.

f: A durable advantage that protects a company from competitors.
c: Deep water around your cave. Enemies see your meat but cannot reach it.

f: Minimum Viable Product — the simplest version you can ship to test if people want it.
c: Ugly first spear. Not pretty, but you can throw it TODAY and see if it hits anything.

f: Changing a startup's core idea when the current one isn't working.
c: Hunt not working. Tribe turns and hunts different animal. No shame — Twitch and Instagram both did this.

f: When a product clicks — people want it, use it, and tell others.
c: Spear finally fits hand perfectly. Humans grab product from you faster than you can make it. Before this: struggle. After this: hold on tight.

f: Evidence that people actually use and want your product (users, revenue, growth).
c: Footprints proving animals actually come to your trap. Investors want to see footprints, not stories.

f: To improve a product in repeated small cycles.
c: Throw spear. See miss. Fix spear. Throw again. Repeat until mammoth falls.

f: Ownership shares in a company.
c: Piece of tribe. Own piece = when tribe gets rich, you get rich.

f: Units of ownership in a company.
c: Tribe cut into many small pieces. Each piece = one share. More pieces you hold, more of tribe is yours.

f: Earning your equity gradually over time (usually 4 years).
c: You get tribe pieces slowly, moon by moon, so you no grab pieces and run away on day two.

f: What a company is judged to be worth.
c: What crowd thinks whole tribe is worth. Often guess. Sometimes very silly guess.

f: The list of who owns what percentage of a company.
c: Stone tablet listing who owns which piece of tribe. Keep tablet clean or big fights later.

f: The first significant money a startup raises.
c: First bag of rocks to plant seed. Buys food while tribe builds first spear.

f: Later, bigger rounds of investment as the company grows.
c: Bigger and bigger rock bags. A = tribe shows promise. B = tribe growing. C = tribe getting huge. Each bag costs more tribe pieces.

f: An investor who puts money into risky startups hoping a few become huge.
c: Rich human who gives rocks to ten baby tribes knowing nine die. Prays tenth becomes mammoth and pays for all.

f: An individual who invests their own money in very early startups.
c: Kind rich human who gives own rocks to baby tribe when nobody else believes.

f: Simple Agreement for Future Equity — a YC-invented way to invest quickly in early startups.
c: Rich human gives rocks NOW, gets tribe pieces LATER when price is decided. Less arguing, faster rocks. YC invented this.

f: A document listing the terms of an investment offer.
c: Stone tablet where rich human writes offer: this many rocks, this many pieces. Read tablet CAREFULLY.

f: Your ownership percentage shrinking as new shares are issued to investors.
c: Every time tribe takes new rocks, your piece of tribe gets thinner. Same soup, more water.

f: Building a company using only your own money and revenue, no investors.
c: Tribe grows on own hunted meat only. No rich humans, no bosses. Slower, but tribe keeps all pieces.

f: How much money a company loses per month.
c: How fast tribe eats rock pile every moon. Eat too fast = tribe dies.

f: How many months of money a company has left at its current burn rate.
c: How many moons until rock pile empty. Short runway = find rocks or die soon. Always know your runway.

f: Money coming in from customers.
c: Rocks flowing INTO cave from happy customers. Not same as profit!

f: Money left over after paying all costs.
c: Rocks left after feeding whole tribe and fixing all spears. Many famous tribes have zero of this for long time.

f: Annual/Monthly Recurring Revenue — predictable subscription income per year/month.
c: Rocks that come back every moon (MRR) or every year (ARR) without new hunting. Investors LOVE rocks that repeat.

f: Percent of revenue left after direct costs of the product.
c: You sell berry for 10 rocks, berry costs 3 rocks to pick. Margin = 7 rocks of every 10. Software tribes keep almost all 10 — that's why everyone wants software tribe.

f: Whether each individual sale/customer makes or loses money.
c: Does ONE trade make rocks or lose rocks? If every delivery loses 2 rocks, doing million deliveries = million times sadder.

f: Customer Acquisition Cost — what it costs to win one new customer.
c: Rocks spent to catch one new customer. Shouting (ads) is expensive.

f: Lifetime Value — total money one customer brings over their whole relationship.
c: All rocks one customer gives tribe over whole friendship. LTV must be much bigger than CAC or tribe slowly dies.

f: The rate at which customers cancel or leave.
c: Customers leaking out of cave like water through cracked pot. High churn = filling pot forever, never full.

f: How well a product keeps its users coming back.
c: Humans keep coming back to your fire. Best sign your fire is actually warm.

f: How fast users or revenue are increasing, usually per week/month.
c: How fast tribe getting bigger. YC says: grow some % every week or something wrong.

f: The one number a company optimizes above all others.
c: One star tribe follows at night. Airbnb: nights slept. Pick wrong star, walk into swamp.

f: The plan for how you'll reach and win customers.
c: Hunt plan: which animal, which forest, which spear, who throws.

f: Free basic version, paid better version.
c: Free berries at cave door. Want SWEET berries? Pay rocks. Free berries bring crowd.

f: Growth driven by people trying and sharing the product itself, not salespeople.
c: Product so good it sells itself. Human uses tool, shows friend, friend wants tool. No shouty sales human needed.

f: Very large company customers.
c: Mammoth-sized customer tribe. Slow to catch (many meetings, many moons), but feeds village all winter.

f: Small and medium businesses as customers.
c: Many small customer tribes. Easy to catch, but each gives small rocks and some die often.

f: Software built deeply for one specific industry.
c: Tool made for exactly ONE kind of tribe (only healers, only builders). Fits that hand perfectly.

f: A private startup valued at $1 billion or more.
c: Magic horse tribe worth billion+ rocks. Rare. Some are real magic, some just good story-tellers.

f: When founders/investors cash out — via acquisition or IPO.
c: Day tribe pieces turn into real rocks. Two doors: bigger tribe buys you, or you go public.

f: One company buying another.
c: Big tribe buys whole small tribe. Small chiefs get rocks, sometimes stay, often wander off after.

f: Initial Public Offering — listing shares on a stock market so anyone can buy them.
c: Tribe pieces sold in big public market. Any human can now own piece. Big drums, big scrutiny.

f: Mergers and Acquisitions — companies combining or buying each other.
c: Tribes eating tribes. Business circle of life.

f: Careful checking of a company before investing or buying it.
c: Rich human sniffs everything in your cave before giving rocks. Counts your bones twice.

f: The slide presentation founders use to raise money.
c: Story-drawings founders show rich humans. Cave paintings that say: big problem, our magic, give rocks.

f: Building in secret before launching publicly.
c: Tribe builds spear hidden in dark cave so enemies no copy. Sometimes secret because spear not ready. Sometimes because spear boring.

f: To upend an old industry with a new, better/cheaper way.
c: New tribe with better spear makes old giant tribes look slow and silly. Old tribes very grumpy about this.

f: Startups built on hard science or engineering breakthroughs (rockets, biology, chips).
c: Not app — REAL hard magic: fire-rocks, space spears, cell factories. Takes many more moons and rocks, but enemy cannot copy in weekend.

f: Application Programming Interface — a way for programs to talk to each other.
c: Magic door between two computer things. App knocks on door with right words, door gives back what app needs. Stripe = famous door for money.

f: Computers you rent over the internet instead of owning.
c: Your stuff lives in someone else's giant computer cave (Amazon's, Google's). You rent space, reach it from anywhere.

f: A computer that runs websites and apps and serves them to users.
c: Strong tireless computer that never sleeps, handing out your website to every visitor all night.

f: Backend = the behind-the-scenes logic and data. Frontend = what users see and click.
c: Frontend = painted cave wall humans look at. Backend = hidden tunnels and fires making it all work.

f: Organized storage for an app's information.
c: Very organized bone pile. Every bone labeled, findable in blink. Lose database = tribe forgets everything.

f: Software whose code is public — anyone can read, use, and improve it.
c: Tribe shows spear recipe to whole world free. Sounds crazy, but world helps sharpen spear, and tribe sells help and extras.

f: A step-by-step recipe a computer follows.
c: Recipe for computer. 'The algorithm' deciding what videos you see = recipe some tribe wrote.

f: Computer systems that do tasks that normally need human thinking.
c: Rock that thinks. Feed it million examples, it learns patterns — talks, draws, writes code. Not alive. Probably.

f: AI trained on huge amounts of text to understand and generate language (like ChatGPT or Claude).
c: Giant word-brain that read almost whole internet. Now predicts next word so well it seems to think. You talking to one when you use ChatGPT.

f: Teaching computers by showing examples instead of writing exact rules.
c: No tell computer rules. Show computer 10,000 pictures of wolf, computer learns 'wolf' by itself.

f: Financial technology — startups improving money services.
c: Shiny rock magic. Making paying, saving, borrowing less painful than old grumpy banks.

f: Digital money and the shared public ledger technology behind it.
c: Internet shiny rocks, counted on public stone tablet thousand tribes hold copies of. No single chief controls tablet. Some think future, some think tulips.

f: Tools that let people build software without programming.
c: Build app by dragging pictures with hands. No need speak computer language. Webflow = famous no-code tribe.

f: The practice/tools of shipping and running software reliably.
c: Tribe craft of shipping code fast without setting village on fire. When village does catch fire — PagerDuty screams.

f: The underlying systems everything else runs on (servers, pipes, payments...).
c: Roads and water pipes of computer world. Boring, invisible, everything dies without it. Boring = great business.

f: Collecting and studying data to make decisions.
c: Counting footprints to know where animals go. Tribes that count, eat. Tribes that guess, starve.

f: A screen summarizing important numbers at a glance.
c: One cave wall showing all tribe numbers at once. Chief looks, knows instantly if tribe healthy or on fire.

f: The process of getting a new user or employee set up and comfortable.
c: Welcoming new human into cave: here fire, here spears, here where we keep snacks. Bad welcome = human leaves forever.

f: Able to grow massively without costs growing as fast.
c: Feed 10 humans or 10 million humans with almost same effort. Software very scalable. Restaurant not.

f: Early evidence that something (product, founder, market) is promising.
c: Smoke on horizon telling you where fire is. Good founder signal, good product signal = investors run toward smoke.
